calendar.matters:
MASTER CALENDAR JURY
Off Calendar Jan-27-2026 Continued To Jan-19-27 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
Off Calendar Feb-24-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT LEONARDO GARCIA MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS

Defendant Leonardo Garcia's motion to quash service of summons is granted.

"The general rule is that an amended complaint that adds a new defendant does not relate back to the date of filing the original complaint and the statute of limitations is applied as of the date the amended complaint is filed, not the date the original complaint is filed. A recognized exception to the general rule is the substitution under section 474 of a new defendant for a fictitious Doe defendant named in the original complaint as to whom a cause of action was stated in the original complaint. If the requirements of section 474 are satisfied, the amended complaint substituting a new defendant for a fictitious Doe defendant filed after the statute of limitations has expired is deemed filed as of the date the original complaint was filed." (Woo v. Superior Court (1999) 75 Cal.App.4th 169, 176 [citation simplified].) However, "[a]bsent compliance with Code of Civil Procedure section 474, service of a summons on a purported Doe defendant is ineffective." (Fireman's Fund Ins. Co. v. Sparks Construction, Inc. (2004) 114 Cal.App.4th 1135, 1145.)

The question here is whether plaintiffs complied with Code of Civil Procedure, section 474, in substituting Garcia for a Doe defendant. Section 474 states in relevant part, "[w]hen the plaintiff is ignorant of the name of a defendant, he must state that fact in the complaint, or the affidavit if the action is commenced by affidavit, and such defendant may be designated in any pleading or proceeding by any name, and when his true name is discovered, the pleading or proceeding must be amended accordingly . . . ." In assessing whether the plaintiff had knowledge of the defendant, the courts asks "whether he knew or reasonably should have known that he had a cause of action" against that party. (Wallis v. Southern Pac. Transportation Co. (1976) 61 Cal.App.3d 782, 786.) "Even under the most liberal construction of section 474, it is established that the plaintiff's ignorance, whether of the defendant's true identity or of the facts giving rise to a cause of action, must be real and not feigned." (Id.)

Here, Garcia presents evidence that he gave his driver's license and insurance information to other drivers on the scene of the accident. (Garcia Dec., para. 6.) In addition, Garcia is identified as the driver of the Tesla in the California Highway Patrol's collision report. (Branch Dec., Ex. A.) Accident reports are available to persons involved in the accident. (Veh. Code, section 20012.) Plaintiffs do not directly meet this evidence but instead aver that they first received the accident report much later. This does not demonstrate their diligence in obtaining readily available information, and the court concludes that they reasonably should have known of Garcia's identity and role in the accident at the time of their complaint. He was therefore not a Doe defendant within the meaning of section 474.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (301/CVA)
